title: Frau Trude
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 43. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The forty-third tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 43), a short and pitiless cautionary tale. A headstrong, prying girl defies her parents’ warning and goes to visit the witch Frau Trude; on the way she meets a black, a green and a blood-red man, and at the window the Devil with a fiery head. Frau Trude reveals that she herself is the witch in her true finery, turns the girl into a block of wood, throws it on the fire, and warms herself by the bright blaze — there is no rescue and no comfort. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as gieng and blutroth (modern ging, blutrot).

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein kleines Mädchen, das war eigensinnig und vorwitzig, und wenn ihm seine Eltern etwas sagten, so gehorchte es nicht: wie konnte es dem gut gehen?
sentences.l: There was once a little girl, who was self-willed and forward, and when its parents told it anything, then it did not obey: how could it go well for it?
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a little girl who was headstrong and prying, and when her parents told her anything, she would not obey; how could that go well for her?
sentences.n: “Es war einmal” is the fixed fairy-tale opening, ‘once upon a time.’ Mädchen is grammatically neuter, so German calls the girl ihm/es (‘to it / it’) all through — read it as ‘her.’ “wie konnte es dem gut gehen?” — literally ‘how could it go well for it?’, the narrator’s grim aside (dem is dative). This tale is the disobedience-punished pattern at its starkest: the warning is given, defied, and the punishment falls with no rescue — rare for Grimm.

sentences.t: Eines Tages sagte es zu seinen Eltern „ich habe so viel von der Frau Trude gehört, ich will einmal zu ihr hingehen: die Leute sagen es sehe so wunderlich bei ihr aus und erzählen es seien so seltsame Dinge in ihrem Hause, da bin ich ganz neugierig geworden.“
sentences.l: One day it said to its parents, “I have heard so much of Frau Trude, I want to go to her for once: the people say it looks so strange at her place and tell that there are such curious things in her house, so I have become quite curious.”
sentences.i: One day she said to her parents, “I have heard so much about Frau Trude; I mean to go and see her for once. People say it looks so strange at her house and tell of such curious things in it, and I have grown quite curious.”
sentences.n: “sehe ... aus” is the separable verb aussehen (‘to look, appear’), and both “sehe” and “seien” stand in subjunctive I — the subjunctive of reported speech (‘people say it looks ... that there are ...’), everywhere in Grimm. “einmal” here softens to ‘for once.’ “von ... gehört” — hören von, to hear of/about; “Hause” carries the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Die Eltern verboten es ihr streng und sagten „die Frau Trude ist eine böse Frau, die gottlose Dinge treibt, und wenn du zu ihr hingehst, so bist du unser Kind nicht mehr.“
sentences.l: The parents forbade it to her strictly and said, “Frau Trude is an evil woman who carries on godless things, and if you go to her, then you are our child no more.”
sentences.i: Her parents strictly forbade it and said, “Frau Trude is a wicked woman who works godless deeds, and if you go to her, you are our child no longer.”
sentences.n: “verboten ... es ihr” — verbieten takes a dative person (ihr, ‘to her’) and accusative thing (es, ‘it’). “gottlose Dinge treiben” — treiben here = to carry on, practise (evil deeds). “wenn ... so” is the ‘if ... then’ frame you meet throughout Grimm.

sentences.t: Aber das Mädchen kehrte sich nicht an das Verbot seiner Eltern und gieng doch zu der Frau Trude.
sentences.l: But the girl did not turn herself to the prohibition of its parents and went to Frau Trude anyway.
sentences.i: But the girl paid no heed to her parents’ prohibition and went to Frau Trude all the same.
sentences.n: “sich an etwas kehren” = to heed, mind something (chiefly in the negative, ‘to pay no heed’). “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); this -ie- spelling recurs in the edition. “doch” here = ‘anyway, all the same.’

sentences.t: Und als es zu ihr kam, fragte die Frau Trude „warum bist du so bleich?“
sentences.l: And when it came to her, Frau Trude asked, “why are you so pale?”
sentences.i: And when she came to her, Frau Trude asked, “Why are you so pale?”

sentences.t: „Ach,“ antwortete es, und zitterte am Leibe, „ich habe mich so erschrocken über das was ich gesehen habe.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” it answered, and trembled in the body, “I have frightened myself so over that which I have seen.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” she answered, trembling all over, “I have been so frightened at what I saw.”
sentences.n: “sich erschrecken” = to be frightened, take fright (reflexive). “am Leibe” — der Leib is the body, so ‘trembled in the body’ = trembled all over (Leibe has the old dative -e). “über das was” = ‘over that which,’ i.e. at what.

sentences.t: „Ich sah auf eurer Stiege einen schwarzen Mann.“
sentences.l: “I saw a black man on your stair.”
sentences.n: die Stiege = staircase, stairs. “eurer” is the old respectful ‘your’ (from Ihr/Euch), which the girl uses to Frau Trude — the same polite plural other Grimm characters use to elders. The three coloured men — black, green, blood-red — are a rising ladder of dread that Frau Trude will explain away one by one with an everyday trade.
der Köhler = charcoal-burner, blackened by his coals — the ordinary explanation for the black man. “Das war” fronts the demonstrative, ‘that was.’
der Jäger = huntsman, who wore green — the plain reading of the green man.

sentences.l: “After that I saw a blood-red man.”
sentences.n: “blutroth” (modern blutrot) = blood-red; the silent -th- is the 1857 spelling, as in Noth, Thür. blutrothen is the inflected accusative.
der Metzger = butcher, red with blood — the everyday explanation for the blood-red man. Three harmless trades; the fourth figure will have no such excuse.

sentences.t: „Ach, Frau Trude, mir grauste, ich sah durchs Fenster und sah Euch nicht, wohl aber den Teufel mit feurigem Kopf.“
sentences.l: “Oh, Frau Trude, horror seized me, I looked through the window and did not see you, but rather the Devil with a fiery head.”
sentences.i: “Oh, Frau Trude, I shuddered with dread — I looked through the window and did not see you, but instead the Devil with his fiery head.”
sentences.n: “mir grauste” — grausen is impersonal with a dative: ‘it filled me with horror, I shuddered’ (mir = ‘to me’). “wohl aber” = ‘but rather, but instead,’ marking the contrast (not you, but the Devil). The fiery-headed Devil breaks the pattern: the first three had harmless trades, but this one Frau Trude will own.

sentences.t: „Oho,“ sagte sie, „so hast du die Hexe in ihrem rechten Schmuck gesehen: ich habe schon lange auf dich gewartet und nach dir verlangt, du sollst mir leuchten.“
sentences.l: “Oho,” she said, “so you have seen the witch in her true finery: I have waited for you a long time already and longed for you — you shall give me light.”
sentences.i: “Oho,” she said, “so you have seen the witch in her proper array; I have long waited for you and longed for you — you shall give me light.”
sentences.n: The turn of the tale: Frau Trude names herself the Hexe (witch), and the Devil’s fiery head was her ‘true finery’ (der Schmuck = adornment, jewels). “rechten” here = ‘true, proper, rightful.’ “auf jemanden warten” = to wait for; “nach jemandem verlangen” = to long for. “du sollst mir leuchten” — leuchten = to shine, give light: the girl is to light the witch, as the next lines make horribly literal.

sentences.t: Da verwandelte sie das Mädchen in einen Holzblock und warf ihn ins Feuer.
sentences.l: Then she transformed the girl into a block of wood and threw it into the fire.
sentences.n: “verwandeln in” (+ accusative) = to transform into. der Holzblock = a block/log of wood; “warf ihn” — ihn (‘it’) is masculine, agreeing with the block, not the girl. No rescue comes: the punishment is instant and final.

sentences.t: Und als er in voller Glut war, setzte sie sich daneben, wärmte sich daran und sprach „das leuchtet einmal hell!“
sentences.l: And when it was in full glow, she seated herself beside it, warmed herself at it, and spoke, “that gives a bright light for once!”
sentences.i: And when it was in full blaze, she sat down beside it, warmed herself at it, and said, “Now that burns bright!”
sentences.n: “er” and “daran” refer to the Holzblock (masculine), the burning girl. die Glut = embers, glowing heat, blaze. “das leuchtet einmal hell” — einmal here is a flavouring ‘now that’s what I call ...’; the witch’s cosy last word ends the tale on its coldest note — no rescue, no moral spoken.
